--

Most people assume every WordPress website is built the same way. It isn’t. One of the questions I get asked the most — usually right after a client tells me what kind of business they run — is “which builder are you going to use for my site?” And honestly, the answer changes every time, because the right tool depends entirely on what the website actually needs to do, not on what I personally prefer.
I’ve spent 6+ years doing web development services for businesses in Dubai, Abu Dhabi, and across the UAE, and in that time I’ve built 250+ websites using Elementor, Bricks Builder, and Crocoblock — sometimes combining them on the same project. Here’s the actual decision-making process I go through, so you understand why your website might end up built one way instead of another.
Elementor: The Right Choice for Straightforward, Design-Flexible Business Sites
Elementor powers over 21 million websites worldwide, and there’s a reason it’s still my default for a large share of client projects. It’s a drag-and-drop builder, which means I can move fast on static pages, custom headers, footers, and archive layouts without writing code for every section.
I’ve personally built 65+ websites on Elementor, and it’s usually my first recommendation when a client needs:
A service-based business website (consultancies, clinics, restaurants, agencies) A site where the design needs to be adjusted frequently A project with a tighter turnaround — some of my Elementor builds, like a recent luxury yacht rental site in Dubai, went from brief to launch in about a week
The tradeoff is that Elementor can get heavier if a site isn’t optimized properly, which is why speed and Core Web Vitals work usually goes hand-in-hand with an Elementor build in my process.
Bricks Builder: When Performance and SEO Come First
Bricks Builder is a fully HTML and CSS-based WordPress theme and builder that launched in 2021, and I’ve been using it since December 2024. I’ve already delivered 55+ websites with it, and it’s become my go-to when a client’s priority is speed and search visibility rather than heavy visual customization.
Because Bricks is lightweight by design, it needs far fewer plugins to function properly, which directly improves Core Web Vitals — something Google actively factors into rankings. I reach for Bricks when a business is in a competitive niche where SEO performance genuinely affects how many leads come through the site, which is common in real estate, logistics, and hospitality projects I’ve handled across the UAE.
Crocoblock: For Websites That Need to Do More Than Show Information
Some websites aren’t just brochures — they’re functional platforms. That’s where Crocoblock comes in. It’s a WordPress toolkit built around JetPlugins that lets me create dynamic, database-driven websites without writing everything from scratch in raw code.
I’ve used Crocoblock since 2022 and built 60+ websites with it, mostly for:
Property listing and rental platforms Booking systems Custom post types and category-based directories
If a client needs users to filter listings, book something, or interact with structured data instead of just reading static content, Crocoblock is almost always part of the answer.
Sometimes the Real Answer Is “All Three, Working Together”
A good example is a freelance job-board platform I redesigned for a Netherlands-based client — I used Elementor for the front-end layout and Crocoblock to power the advanced filtering system, job cards, and separate client/freelancer dashboards. Neither tool alone would have covered everything the project needed.
This is really the core of how I approach WordPress website development: the builder is a means to an end, not the goal itself. What actually matters is whether the finished site loads fast, ranks well, is easy for the business owner to manage, and converts visitors into paying customers.
What This Means If You’re Planning a Website
If you’re a business owner in Dubai, Abu Dhabi, or anywhere else in the UAE thinking about a new site or a rebuild, it’s worth asking whoever you hire why they’re choosing a specific builder for your project — not just what the finished design will look like. A mismatched tool can leave you with a site that looks fine but loads slowly, or one that’s fast but can’t handle the dynamic features your business actually needs, like bookings, listings, or filters.
I’ve broken down these exact services, along with speed optimization, Figma-to-WordPress conversion, and hosting/domain support, on my services page. You can also see real examples of Elementor, Bricks, and Crocoblock projects in my portfolio, or read more about how I got here in my about me page.
If you’re currently deciding how to approach your own website — or wondering why an existing one isn’t performing — feel free to reach out and I’m happy to walk through what would actually make sense for your specific business.
